CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text:
Abstract
While deploying large language models on edge devices promises low-latency and privacy-preserving AI services, it is hindered by limited device resources. Although pipeline parallelism facilitates distributed inference, existing approaches often ignore the cold-start latency caused by on-demand mode
**Keywords:** large language models, mobile edge computing, cold start latency, pipeline parallelism

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: Abstract

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: While deploying large language models on edge devices promises low-latency and privacy-preserving AI services, it is hindered by limited device resources. Although pipeline parallelism facilitates distributed inference, existing approaches often ignore the cold-start latency caused by on-demand model loading. In this paper, we propose a latency-aware scheduling framework that overlaps model loading with computation and communication to minimize total inference latency. Based on device and model parameters, the framework dynamically adjusts layer partitioning and allocation to effectively hide loading time, thereby eliminating as many idle periods as possible. We formulate the problem as a Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Program and design an efficient dynamic programming algorithm to optimize model partitioning and device assignment. Experimental results show that the proposed method significantly reduces cold-start latency compared to baseline strategies.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: **Keywords:** large language models, mobile edge computing, cold start latency, pipeline parallelism

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: 1 INTRODUCTION

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: Recent years have witnessed a paradigm shift in artificial intelligence (AI), driven by the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) and generative diffusion models. These models exhibit unprecedented capabilities, revolutionizing domains from natural language processing to creative content generation. As their influence extends across diverse aspects of digital lives, there is a growing demand to move beyond cloud-centric deployments and embed these powerful AI functionalities directly into personal computing, particularly onto mobile devices such as smartphones. On-device inference holds the promise of a new wave of applications, offering advantages such as real-time responsiveness, enhanced user privacy through local data processing, and highly personalized user experiences.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: However, realizing this vision poses a fundamental challenge: the massive computational and memory requirements of state-of-the-art models sharply contrast with the limited resources of mobile devices. A single large model can easily exceed the available RAM and processing power of a typical smartphone, rendering local execution impractical. To address this mismatch, distributed inference has emerged as a promising paradigm. The key idea is to partition a monolithic model into smaller segments, or “shards,” and distribute them across a network of collaborating mobile devices. By organizing these shards into a pipeline parallel workflow, the system can process inference requests in an assembly-line fashion, leveraging the aggregate capabilities of multiple devices to overcome individual hardware constraints.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:

Pipeline Parallelism (PP), a fundamental model parallelism strategy, has been widely adopted for both the training and inference of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs). In cloud-centric server environments, a variety of distributed inference strategies have been developed to optimize performance and resourc
----

text: 1.1 Background

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: The cold start problem in personal devices, where long idle periods between inference requests are typical, can lead to substantial latency as each task may require loading the model from storage into memory from scratch. This latency directly impacts user experience, which is a crucial challenge that this paper aims to address.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

The key idea is to overlap model loading with ongoing computation and communication phases within the pipeline. By str

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: 1.2 Contributions

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: To tackle the cold-start challenge in wireless distributed collaborative inference, this paper proposes a latency-aware pipeline scheduling algorithm tailored for edge environments. The key idea is to overlap model loading with ongoing computation and communication phases within the pipeline. By strategically scheduling the on-demand loading of downstream shards in parallel with the execution of upstream stages, the proposed method effectively hides the loading latency, reduces pipeline stalls, and minimizes end-to-end inference latency.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows:
- Systematic identification and analysis of the cold-start latency issue in mobile distributed cooperative inference scenarios, along with a mathematical model of the latency introduced by the cold start of large models.
- Design and implementation of a dynamic programming (DP)-based scheduling algorithm that jointly determines optimal layer partitioning and device assignment for both loading and computation, aiming to minimize end-to-end inference latency.
- Validation of the approach through extensive experiments, demonstrating substantial reductions in user-perceived latency and improved pipeline efficiency compared to conventional strategies that decouple loading from computation.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

- **Section IV** proposes the DP algorithm tailored for latency optimization.
- **Section V** shows experimental res

1. Introduction
In the edge deployment scenarios of interest, the number of model layers typically exceeds the number of available devices, i.e., \( L \geq K \). Each layer \( l \in L \) is characterized by a triple \( \phi_l = \{W_l, A_l, D_l\} \), where \( W_l \) is the computational workload in floating point op
The model is a Transformer-based LLM that employs a multi-head attention mechanism with Grouped Query Attention (GQA) and a Swish-Gated Linear Unit (SwiGLU)-based feed-forward network (FFN). Both activations and parameters are represented using the bfloat16 (bf16) format.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: 2.1 Computational Resources

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: We focus on low-workload inference scenarios, which are typical in edge deployments. In such settings, the actual utilization of a mobile device is often substantially lower than its theoretical peak performance or the average utilization observed during large-scale training. Depending on factors such as batch size and input sequence length, utilization rates can vary widely, typically ranging from 1% to 40%. As a result, it is important to account for workload size when evaluating the effective computational capability.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: To capture this behavior, we model utilization using a saturating exponential function:

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems
CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: where \( U(t) \) denotes the utilization rate as a function of the workload \( t \), \( a \) represents the maximum achievable utilization, and \( b \) controls the growth rate. The parameters \( a \) and \( b \) are derived from empirical observations in preliminary experiments.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: Let \( c^* \) denote the theoretical peak computational capability of device \( k \). The effective computational capability, accounting for workload-dependent utilization, is then given by:

CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

Since this metric is generally stable and the underlying hardware-level read process lies outside the scope of our optimi

Since this metric is generally stable and the underlying hardware-level read process lies outside the scope of our optimization, we abstract it using standardized, canonical values.

text:
2.3 Deployment Framework
This paper adopts a pipeline parallelism strategy to deploy the model across K heterogeneous mobile devices, as depicted in **Fig. 2**. The large model’s L layers are partitioned into N contiguous, non-overlapping segments (hereafter referred to as partitions), where \(1 \leq N \leq K\). Each partit
To formalize the partitioning scheme, we define a triplet \(\{k_n, s_n, e_n\}\) for each partition \(n \in N \equiv \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}\). Here, \(k_n \in K\) denotes the index of the device assigned to the n-th partition, while \(s_n \in L\) and \(e_n \in L\) represent the starting and ending layer
The allocation scheme must satisfy the following constraints. First, devices assigned to different partitions must be unique:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: 2.3 Deployment Framework

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: This paper adopts a pipeline parallelism strategy to deploy the model across K heterogeneous mobile devices, as depicted in **Fig. 2**. The large model’s L layers are partitioned into N contiguous, non-overlapping segments (hereafter referred to as partitions), where \(1 \leq N \leq K\). Each partition is assigned exclusively to a single device for execution.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: To formalize the partitioning scheme, we define a triplet \(\{k_n, s_n, e_n\}\) for each partition \(n \in N \equiv \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}\). Here, \(k_n \in K\) denotes the index of the device assigned to the n-th partition, while \(s_n \in L\) and \(e_n \in L\) represent the starting and ending layer indices of the partition, respectively.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: The allocation scheme must satisfy the following constraints. First, devices assigned to different partitions must be unique:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
\forall n, n' \in N, n = n' \Rightarrow k_n = k_{n'} \quad (13)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: Second, the partitioning must be contiguous and comprehensive, covering all layers of the model:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
s_1 = 1, \quad e_N = L \quad (14)
\]
\[
\forall n \in N, \quad s_n \leq e_n \quad (15)
\]
\[
\forall n \in N \setminus \{1\}, \quad s_n = e_{n-1} + 1 \quad (16)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: Before the inference task begins, device \(k_n\) stores the model parameters for its assigned layers (from \(s_n\) to \(e_n\)) in its local storage. When an inference request arrives, it triggers a cold start process consisting of three main stages: model loading, activation transfer, and forward computation.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: During pipeline execution, the start time \(t_{start}\) for device \(k_n\) depends on two factors: the completion of its own model loading and the computation finish time \(t_{finish}\) of the preceding device \(k_{n-1}\). Due to the limited I/O bandwidth of mobile devices, we consider that model loading and transmission are performed sequentially, that is, a device cannot begin communicating until it has completed loading the model. The model loading start time and the output transmission completion time of device \(k_n\) are given by:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
t_{k_n}^{start} = \max(t_{k_n}^{load \, finish}, t_{k_{n-1}}^{finish}) \quad (17)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
t_{finish} = t_{start} + t_{comm} + t_{comp} \quad (18)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: Here, \(t_{finish}\) is defined as 0. The terms \(t_{load}\), \(t_{comm}\), and \(t_{comp}\) represent model loading latency, activation transfer latency, and computation latency of device \(k_n\), respectively. These latencies are calculated as follows:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
t_{load} = \frac{\sum_{l=s_n}^{e_n} P_l}{r_{k_n}} \quad (19)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks
Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
t_{comp} = \sum_{l=s_n}^{e_n} W^l \quad (21)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: Additionally, to account for device memory constraints, we have:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
m_{k_n} \geq \max \left( A_l + \sum_{l=s_n}^{e_n} P_l \right) \quad (22)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
3 Problem Formulation
In this section, we first highlight the necessity of optimization by analyzing the performance bottlenecks of a baseline deployment strategy. We then formulate a combinatorial optimization problem aimed at minimizing the total cold-start latency:
\[
T = t_{finish} \quad (23)
\]
To illustrate the core idea behind the proposed method, we first consider a baseline method in which the model is evenly partitioned into K segments and deployed across K homogeneous devices. As shown in **Fig. 2**, this strategy parallelizes model loading across devices but fails to exploit pipelin

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: 3 Problem Formulation

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: In this section, we first highlight the necessity of optimization by analyzing the performance bottlenecks of a baseline deployment strategy. We then formulate a combinatorial optimization problem aimed at minimizing the total cold-start latency:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
T = t_{finish} \quad (23)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: To illustrate the core idea behind the proposed method, we first consider a baseline method in which the model is evenly partitioned into K segments and deployed across K homogeneous devices. As shown in **Fig. 2**, this strategy parallelizes model loading across devices but fails to exploit pipelining opportunities between model loading and computation. Specifically, each device \(k_n\) must wait for its predecessor \(k_{n-1}\) to complete both its computation and activation transfer before beginning its own computation. This results in idle time which can be quantified as:

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
\[
t_{wait} = \max(0, t_{finish} - t_{load}) \quad (24)
\]

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: To eliminate this bottleneck, the proposed algorithm aims to align the computation completion time of a predecessor.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

- There are two kinds of bubbles: obstructive and non-obstructive.
- The bubble after loading is an inevitable idle time while the bubble after computation can be filled by other requests.
- The former is what we aim to eliminate.
- In all strategies, the pipeline proceeds from top to bottom.
- **Ideal Single Device** refers to assigning all model layers to the most powerful device in the system, assuming no RAM constraints.
- **Even** denotes a uniform (even) distribution.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems
CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text:
Optimization Problem (P0)
The optimization problem (P0) is a complex Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Program (MINLP). Its complexity arises primarily from two factors:
1. The presence of integer decision variables, including the number of partitions \( N \), the layer boundaries \( s_n, e_n \), and the combinatorial device assignments \( k_n \).
2. The nonlinearities introduced by the use of max and min operators in the timing constraints.
Overall, P0 is an NP-hard problem. Despite its theoretical intractability, we observe that in practical deployment scenarios, the problem size is often moderate, e.g., the number of available devices \( K \) and model layers \( L \) typically satisfy \( K \leq 20, L \leq 100 \). More importantly, th

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: Optimization Problem (P0)

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: The optimization problem (P0) is a complex Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Program (MINLP). Its complexity arises primarily from two factors:

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text:
1. The presence of integer decision variables, including the number of partitions \( N \), the layer boundaries \( s_n, e_n \), and the combinatorial device assignments \( k_n \).
2. The nonlinearities introduced by the use of max and min operators in the timing constraints.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: Overall, P0 is an NP-hard problem. Despite its theoretical intractability, we observe that in practical deployment scenarios, the problem size is often moderate, e.g., the number of available devices \( K \) and model layers \( L \) typically satisfy \( K \leq 20, L \leq 100 \). More importantly, the problem exhibits a clear optimal substructure property, meaning that an optimal solution to the full problem can be built from optimal solutions to its subproblems. This property makes the problem well-suited for a Dynamic Programming (DP) approach.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: Accordingly, we design a DP algorithm capable of computing the exact optimal solution within a reasonable runtime, as detailed in the following section.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems
CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text:
Proposed DP Algorithm
In the previous section, we formulated the problem as a MINLP, which involves jointly determining the model partitioning and computational task assignment. text:
Algorithm 1 Optimal Pipeline Scheduling
O((KL)ᴷ ). This level of complexity makes the approach infeasible for practical scenarios. Fortunately, the problem exhibits two key properties that make it well-suited for a DP solution:
- Optimal Substructure: A globally optimal solution for partitioning and scheduling L layers across K devices necessarily includes optimal solutions to its subproblems, such as scheduling the first j layers (where j < L) across a subset of devices S ⊂ K.
- Overlapping Subproblems: In the recurs
Algorithm 1 Optimal Pipeline Scheduling
O((KL)ᴷ ). This level of complexity makes the approach infeasible for practical scenarios. Fortunately, the problem exhibits two key properties that make it well-suited for a DP solution:

- Optimal Substructure: A globally optimal solution for partitioning and scheduling L layers across K devices necessarily includes optimal solutions to its subproblems, such as scheduling the first j layers (where j < L) across a subset of devices S ⊂ K.
- Overlapping Subproblems: In the recursive construction of the global solution, certain subproblems, e.g., computing the minimum completion time for scheduling the first j layers on a given subset of devices recur multiple times. DP mitigates redundant computations by storing and reusing these intermediate results.

4.1 DP State Definition
We begin by defining the following utility functions for time cost and memory usage, which can be precomputed prior to executing the main DP algorithm to enhance efficiency:
To formulate the subproblems and define the state transitions in our DP approach, we represent each state as a triplet (S, j, d), where S ⊂ K is the set of devices used so far, j ∈ {1, 2,..., L} is the index of the last layer assigned, and d ∈ S is the device to which the final segment (ending at la
The DP state value, denoted as DP(S, j, d), represents the minimum total completion time required to partition and schedule the first j layers (i.e., layers 1 to j) onto the device set S, with the final segment processed by device d.
4.1 DP State Definition
We begin by defining the following utility functions for time cost and memory usage, which can be precomputed prior to executing the main DP algorithm to enhance efficiency:

To formulate the subproblems and define the state transitions in our DP approach, we represent each state as a triplet (S, j, d), where S ⊂ K is the set of devices used so far, j ∈ {1, 2,..., L} is the index of the last layer assigned, and d ∈ S is the device to which the final segment (ending at layer j) is assigned. In our implementation, the set S is represented by a bitmask, an integer where the k-th (binary) bit is 1 if device d is in the set, and 0 otherwise.

The DP state value, denoted as DP(S, j, d), represents the minimum total completion time required to partition and schedule the first j layers (i.e., layers 1 to j) onto the device set S, with the final segment processed by device d.

4.2 State Transition Recurrence
To compute the value of the DP state DP(S, j, d), we consider all possible predecessor states corresponding to valid partitions of the first j layers. Suppose the final segment assigned to device d begins at layer i + 1, where 1 ≤ i < j). This implies that the first i layers (i.e., layer 1 thro
4.2 State Transition Recurrence

To compute the value of the DP state DP(S, j, d), we consider all possible predecessor states corresponding to valid partitions of the first j layers. Suppose the final segment assigned to device d begins at layer i + 1, where 1 ≤ i < j). This implies that the first i layers (i.e., layer 1 through i) have already been scheduled on the reduced device set S′ = S \ {d},

The simulation environment is configured as a typical personal Wi-Fi network, consisting of one AP and four heterogeneous computing devices. For model parameters, we adopt the settings of the Qwen3-14B model. The detailed simulation configurations, including the computational, I/O characteristics, and communication bandwidths of each device, are summarized in Table 1.

text: 5.2 Performance Evaluation

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: To comprehensively validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, we compare it against three baseline strategies:

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text:
- **Ideal Single Device**: An idealized reference in which all model layers are deployed on the most powerful device (Device 1), assumed to have unlimited GPU memory.
- **Even**: A naive strategy that evenly partitions model layers among the four devices, ignoring performance heterogeneity. Allocation priority is given to stronger devices.
- **Heuristic**: A performance-aware strategy that allocates layers based on a metric defined as the harmonic mean of a device’s compute capability and disk read speed. Layers are proportionally distributed according to this metric, again prioritizing stronger devices.

Analysis

The following analysis reveals a key trade-off underlying these results: I/O parallelization versus computational heterogen

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: Analysis

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: The method reduces latency by 8% to 50% compared to the baselines. Even against the best-performing baseline at each token length, it delivers an average improvement of 17.43%. The following analysis reveals a key trade-off underlying these results: I/O parallelization versus computational heterogeneity.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
1) Limitations of Baseline Strategies
In short-token scenarios (e.g., ≤ 2048 tokens), the Even and Heuristic strategies perform reasonably well by parallelizing model loading during the I/O-intensive phase, as illustrated in Fig. 4(a) and Fig. 4(c). However, as token length increases, computation becomes the dominant bottleneck, reveali

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: 1) Limitations of Baseline Strategies

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text: In short-token scenarios (e.g., ≤ 2048 tokens), the Even and Heuristic strategies perform reasonably well by parallelizing model loading during the I/O-intensive phase, as illustrated in Fig. 4(a) and Fig. 4(c). However, as token length increases, computation becomes the dominant bottleneck, revealing the limitations of these static allocation strategies as depicted in Fig. 4(b) and Fig. 4(d). Because they fail to consider computational heterogeneity, the weakest device often becomes a pipeline “straggler,” significantly increasing overall latency. In contrast, the Ideal Single Device exhibits the opposite behavior: although it suffers from higher latency in the I/O phase due to lack of parallelism, its strong computational power enables it to outperform the imbalanced Even and Heuristic strategies in compute-intensive phases.

section_path: Journal of Communications and Information Networks

text:
2) Adaptive Advantage of the Proposed DP
The Proposed DP algorithm effectively addresses the trade-off between I/O parallelization and computational heterogeneity.

Dynamic Layer Deployment
The algorithm dynamically optimizes layer deployment based on the input token length, which determines the expected computational workload. Under low-load conditions, the algorithm favors a more balanced layer distribution to maximize I/O parallelism, as shown in Fig. 5(a). Conversely, under high-lo
In summary, by accurately modeling and dynamically optimizing computation, communication, and I/O overheads, the proposed DP algorithm effectively adapts to device heterogeneity, enabling it to achieve near-optimal performance across diverse workload conditions.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: Dynamic Layer Deployment

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: The algorithm dynamically optimizes layer deployment based on the input token length, which determines the expected computational workload. Under low-load conditions, the algorithm favors a more balanced layer distribution to maximize I/O parallelism, as shown in Fig. 5(a). Conversely, under high-load conditions, it strategically assigns more compute-intensive layers to the most powerful devices, preventing weaker devices from becoming bottlenecks and fully leveraging the system’s heterogeneous computing resources, as illustrated in Fig. 5(c). This shift in allocation strategy with increasing load is clearly visible in Fig. 5: as the load grows, the distribution becomes more skewed, with a larger proportion of layers assigned to stronger devices.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: In summary, by accurately modeling and dynamically optimizing computation, communication, and I/O overheads, the proposed DP algorithm effectively adapts to device heterogeneity, enabling it to achieve near-optimal performance across diverse workload conditions.

----

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: 6 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: In this paper, we tackle the challenge of inference cold-start latency in wireless networks composed of heterogeneous devices. We propose a novel dynamic programming–based layer allocation algorithm to minimize latency. By carefully modeling computation, communication, and I/O overheads, our approach effectively balances these factors to fully leverage the parallel processing capabilities of multiple devices. Experimental results demonstrate that our algorithm significantly outperforms several baseline strategies in reducing end-to-end latency.

section_path: CSGO: Generalized Optimization for Cold Start in Wireless Collaborative Edge LLM Systems

text: Looking forward, a key direction for future work is the seamless integration of our cold-start optimization with strategies tailored for the steady-state, high-throughput inference phase. Designing a dynamic and adaptive transition mechanism between these phases is a critical challenge for achieving comprehensive, lifecycle-aware latency minimization. Our future research will focus on developing a unified optimization strategy.
